mappings.rationale: Provides an overview of the discipline whose history is the subject of this entire section.
Philosophical method has developed throughout the history of philosophy, from Socratic method (A.1.b.i) through modern approaches.
Section A.1 covers ancient Greek and Roman philosophy from the Pre-Socratics through Hellenistic philosophy.
Section A.1.a covers the Pre-Socratic philosophers including cosmology, metaphysics of matter, and Pythagorean number theory.
Section A.1.b.i covers Socrates's ethical concerns, the Socratic method, and his influence.
Section A.1.b.ii covers Plato's dialogues on politics, ethics, metaphysics, epistemology, and cosmology.
Section A.1.b.iii covers Aristotle's metaphysics, logic, epistemology, biology, ethics, and politics.
Section A.1.c.i covers Stoic philosophy from Zeno through Chrysippus and its role during the late Roman Republic and empire.
Section A.1.c.ii covers Epicurean philosophy about the universe, pleasure, and relationship to the gods.
Section A.1.c.iii covers the Skeptics including Pyrrhon's teaching about uncertainty in knowing.
Section A.2.a.i covers Augustine's use of Neoplatonist thought and his doctrine of man.
Section A.2.a.i specifically mentions Anselm's proofs of the existence of God.
Section A.2.c covers Aquinas's synthesis of Aristotelianism and Christian theology.
Section A.2 covers philosophy in the Middle Ages from the patristic period through late medieval thought.
Section A.2.b covers the contribution of Arabic philosophy and Islamic philosophers' role in transmitting Aristotle.
Section A.2.b covers the teaching of Maimonides as part of the contribution of Jewish philosophy.
Section A.2.b covers Islamic philosophy, and C.6 covers Islamic philosophy as philosophy associated with religion.
Section A.1.c.v covers Jewish and Christian philosophy during the Hellenistic Age, and A.2.a covers the patristic period.
Section A.3.a.i specifically names Machiavelli's views on political power in Renaissance philosophy.
Hobbes is named in A.3.a.i (political theory) and A.3.b.i (Empiricist tradition).
Section A.3.b.ii covers Descartes's antiempirical metaphysics and dualism.
Section A.3.b.ii names Spinoza in the Rationalist tradition.
Section A.3.b.ii names Leibniz in the Rationalist tradition.
Section A.3.c.i covers Locke's inquiry into the origin and nature of reason, and A.3.c.iii covers freedom and equality.
Section A.3.c.i covers Hume's science of man as part of Enlightenment epistemology.
Section A.3.c.i covers Kant's critical examination of reason during the Enlightenment.
Section A.3.c.iii covers Rousseau's concern with freedom and equality of citizens.
Section A.3.d.i covers Hegel's Idealism as part of the resurgence of the metaphysical spirit.
Section A.3.d.ii covers the dialectical Materialism of Marx and Engels, and A.3.e.ii covers developments in Marxist thought.
Section A.3.d.iii covers Kierkegaard's preoccupation with states of consciousness.
Section A.3.d.iii covers Schopenhauer's doctrine of cosmic will.
Section A.3.d.iii mentions Nietzsche as part of the 19th-century reaction against Rationalism.
Section A.3.d.ii covers J.S. Mill's theory of knowledge and ethics in the empirical tradition.
Wittgenstein is central to 20th-century philosophy, relevant to developments in A.3.e.
Russell is a major 20th-century philosopher relevant to A.3.e developments in logic and analytic philosophy.
Heidegger's existential and phenomenological thought is relevant to 20th-century philosophy in A.3.e.
Arendt is a major 20th-century social and political philosopher relevant to A.3.e.i.
Adorno is a major 20th-century critical theorist relevant to independent social philosophies in A.3.e.i.
Habermas is a major 20th-century social philosopher relevant to A.3.e.i.
Foucault is a major 20th-century philosopher relevant to developments in A.3.e.
Derrida is a major 20th-century philosopher relevant to Continental philosophy developments in A.3.e.
Existentialism's roots in Kierkegaard (A.3.d.iii) and 20th-century development are covered in this section.
Continental philosophy covers major traditions from phenomenology through poststructuralism relevant to A.3.e.
German philosophy spans the Rationalist tradition (A.3.b), Idealism (A.3.d.i), and 20th-century developments.
French philosophy covers the Enlightenment (A.3.c) through existentialism and structuralism.
Analytic philosophy is a major 20th-century tradition encompassing logical analysis and linguistic philosophy.
Section B.1 covers Indian philosophy from early Hindu and Buddhist thought through the schools of Vedanta.
Section B.2.a covers Confucianism as a classical Chinese philosophical school, and B.2.c covers Neo-Confucianism.
Section B.1.a covers Buddhist sacred literature, and C.2 covers Buddhist philosophy.
The Buddha's teachings are foundational to Buddhist philosophy in B.1.a and B.3.a.
Section B.1 covers Hindu philosophical thought and C.1 covers Hindu philosophy.
Tibetan Buddhism represents a tradition within Buddhist philosophy covered in B.1 and C.2.
Buddhist ethics relates to ethical and political thought in B.1.b and C.2.
Section B.2.d covers the effects of Maoism on 20th-century Chinese philosophy.
Voltaire is a major Enlightenment thinker relevant to A.3.c on philosophy in the Age of Reason.
Tocqueville's political philosophy is relevant to social and political philosophy in A.3.c.iii.
Adam Smith's philosophical contributions to moral philosophy and political economy relate to Enlightenment philosophy (A.3.c.iii).
Montaigne exemplifies Renaissance humanism (A.3.a.ii) with renewed interest in ancient Skepticism and Stoicism.
Simone Weil is a 20th-century philosopher engaging with ethics, politics, and metaphysics in A.3.e.
Feminist philosophy is a significant 20th-century development relevant to A.3.e.i.
Ethical thought is a major thread throughout the history of philosophy from Socrates (A.1.b.i) through Mill (A.3.d.ii).
Section A.3.c.ii-iii covers developments in religious philosophy and the effect of scientific discoveries on philosophical thought.
Political philosophy is a major thread from Renaissance political theory (A.3.a.i) through Enlightenment social thought (A.3.c.iii).
Epistemological issues are central to A.3.c.i covering Locke, Berkeley, Hume, and Kant.
Section A.3.d.iii covers Kierkegaard's preoccupation with states of consciousness, and consciousness is a recurring philosophical theme.
